bibliographic_data.title: Semiparametric empirical likelihood inference with estimating equations under density ratio models
bibliographic_data.abstract: We consider statistical inference under two-sample DRM with additional parameters defined through and/or auxiliary information expressed as estimating equations. We examine the asymptotic properties of the maximum empirical likelihood estimators (MELEs) of the unknown parameters in the DRMs and establish the chi-square limiting distributions for the empirical likelihood ratio (ELR) statistics. We show that the asymptotic variance of the MELEs of the unknown parameters does not decrease if one estimating equation is dropped. Similar properties are obtained for inferences on the cumulative distribution function and quantiles of each of the populations involved. We also propose an ELR test for the validity and usefulness of the auxiliary information.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper develops new semiparametric inference procedures for parameters under a density ratio model with additional estimating equations, and proposes an ELR test for the validity of auxiliary information.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors use maximum empirical likelihood estimators (MELEs) to estimate unknown parameters in the DRM. They establish chi-square limiting distributions for the empirical likelihood ratio statistics and propose a new testing procedure on the validity of auxiliary information expressed as estimating equations.
outcomes: Developed semiparametric inference procedures under density ratio models with additional estimating equations
Proposed an ELR test for validating auxiliary information
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper focuses on statistical inference using a semiparametric density ratio model (DRM) to combine information from two populations. The authors develop new and general inference procedures for parameters under the DRM with additional estimating equations, and propose an empirical likelihood ratio test for validating auxiliary information. The DRM is flexible and can include various commonly used statistical models. The paper also discusses how auxiliary information can be incorporated through unbiased estimating equations to improve estimation efficiency.
